marketOverview: Global markets remain gripped by the Third Gulf War narrative as the US-Israeli conflict with Iran enters its second week. The Strait of Hormuz disruption continues to dominate price action, with WTI crude holding above $90/bbl after a $13.50 surge last week. The key macro shift: JPY has failed as a haven due to Japan's extreme energy import dependence (90%+ from the Middle East), while CAD has emerged as the G10 outperformer as a net energy exporter. USD retains its liquidity premium but faces a new headwind from a shock NFP miss (-92k vs +59k exp). The week's critical data — US CPI (Wed), US PCE + GDP (Fri), UK GDP (Fri), and CAD Jobs (Fri) — will determine whether the stagflation narrative intensifies or the labor market damage forces a Fed pivot.

currencies.name: US Dollar
currencies.rationale: USD retains its safe-haven and liquidity premium as the dominant G10 reserve currency during the Gulf War shock, but the bullish case has become more conditional after February NFP showed a shock -92k print (vs +59k exp) and unemployment rose to 4.4%. The energy exporter advantage — US terms-of-trade benefit while EUR and JPY face crippling import costs — keeps the Dollar supported against the basket, and war-driven inflation (ISM Manufacturing Prices Paid 70.5, highest since mid-2022) limits the Fed's ability to ease quickly. CPI on Wednesday and PCE/GDP on Friday are the key tests: a hot CPI would reinforce the stagflation bid, while soft data could accelerate the market's repricing toward US slowdown.
drivers: Haven and liquidity premium: dominant reserve currency in stress
Energy exporter advantage: US terms-of-trade benefit vs EUR/JPY
NFP shock: -92k (vs +59k exp), unemployment 4.4% — limits bullish conviction
ISM Manufacturing Prices Paid 70.5 (highest since mid-2022, stagflation signal)
Warsh Fed Chair nomination signals future hawkish leadership shift
CPI (WED) + PCE/GDP/JOLTS (FRI) are key data tests

currencies.name: Euro
currencies.rationale: EUR remains under structural pressure as the Eurozone's critical vulnerability as a net energy importer is fully exposed. QatarEnergy's halt of LNG production sent Dutch TTF gas prices up 40% intraday, and the Eurozone Flash CPI surprise (1.9% vs 1.7% exp, Core 2.4%) was read through a stagflation lens rather than as a currency positive. ECB officials have shifted from cuts to discussing potential hikes, but the market is treating higher energy prices as a growth problem first — leaving EUR stranded between firmer front-end pricing and a deteriorating real income outlook. German Final CPI on Wednesday is the only domestic data release; the bigger signal remains whether gas stress and broader risk sentiment keep crowding out the rates support.
drivers: QatarEnergy LNG halt: Dutch TTF gas +40% intraday, Energy Crisis 2.0
Stagflation trap: CPI 1.9% (hot) but market reads it as growth negative
ECB repricing: from cuts to 75% probability of hike by year-end
Trump threatened trade actions against Spain (military base dispute)
EUR/USD fell ~2.5 cents on the week (1.1800 to 1.1546)
German Final CPI (WED) only domestic release this week

currencies.name: British Pound
currencies.rationale: Sterling surprised to the upside last week as the collapse of near-term BoE rate cut expectations became the dominant driver, overriding the UK's energy importer vulnerability. Markets effectively priced out BoE easing for all of 2026 after the energy shock, sending 2-year Gilt yields above 4.00% in their biggest daily rise since May 2025. While the Spring Statement downgraded 2026 growth to 1.1% (from 1.4%) and Fitch warned of fiscal weakening, the relative rates channel proved more powerful than the growth headwinds. The risk to the weak bullish call is Governor Bailey's speech on Thursday — if he sounds more relaxed about inflation, the gilt yield support could fade quickly.
drivers: BoE easing priced out for all of 2026: 2-year Gilt yield above 4.00%
Relative rates support: gilt yields rose hard vs peers
Spring Statement: 2026 growth downgraded to 1.1% (from 1.4%)
Fitch warning: UK fiscal position weakening, energy sensitivity
Iran drone strike on RAF Cyprus base: early week GBP headwind
BoE Gov Bailey Speech (THU) + GDP (FRI) are key catalysts

currencies.name: Japanese Yen
currencies.rationale: JPY suffered a haven failure last week as Japan's extreme energy import dependence (90%+ of crude from the Middle East) overwhelmed the usual defensive bid. USD/JPY broke above 158.00 even as US yields fell after the NFP miss — a clear sign the energy channel is now the dominant driver. BoJ communication remained mixed: Ueda signaled wages are rising broadly, but Himino struck a cautious tone on Friday, reinforcing that policy stays accommodative despite the oil shock. Intervention warnings from Finance Minister Suzuki were largely ignored by the market. Final GDP on Monday and PPI on Tuesday are the key domestic releases.
drivers: Haven failure: energy importer shock overwhelmed defensive bid
Japan imports 90%+ of crude from Middle East — direct terms-of-trade hit
USD/JPY above 158.00 despite falling US yields (energy channel dominant)
BoJ Himino: monetary conditions must remain accommodative
BoJ March hike ruled out; April remains possible
Final GDP (MON) + PPI (TUE) key domestic data

currencies.name: Australian Dollar
currencies.rationale: AUD sits at a genuine crossroads: a strong Q4 GDP beat (0.8% QoQ vs 0.6% exp) briefly revived RBA rate hike speculation and showed the currency can recover even in a difficult external backdrop, but China's lowest GDP growth target since 1991 (4.5-5%) and new BHP cargo restrictions weighed on the resource sector outlook. The high-beta risk proxy role remains the key vulnerability — AUD was one of the first currencies sold in the initial panic but also one of the first to recover. PMT rates this as a mixed/weak bullish skew, but the external backdrop is still fragile enough to keep a neutral stance appropriate. Inflation Expectations on Thursday is the only domestic release.
drivers: Q4 GDP 0.8% QoQ (vs 0.6% exp) — strong beat, RBA hike speculation revived
China GDP target 4.5-5%: lowest since 1991, structural demand concern
New BHP cargo restrictions: direct hit to iron ore/mining sector
Gold sell-off on Poland gold reserve reports dragged AUD lower
High-beta risk proxy: first sold in panic, first to recover
Inflation Expectations (THU) only domestic release

currencies.name: Canadian Dollar
currencies.rationale: CAD was the clear G10 outperformer last week, breaking away from broader risk sentiment as the petro-currency trade dominated. WTI surged $13.50/bbl to above $90, creating a massive positive terms-of-trade shock for Canada as a net energy exporter — the exact opposite of the EUR and JPY story. BoC Deputy Governor Kozicki delivered a hawkish surprise, warning that policy may need to be tightened even when the economy is weak if inflation is supply-driven. The Ivey PMI jumped to 56.6 (vs 51.1 exp) on Friday, contrasting sharply with the US NFP miss. The Canadian Jobs Report on Friday is the key test — a strong print would extend the bullish case, while a miss could dial it back toward weak bullish.
drivers: Petro-currency trade: WTI +$13.50/bbl to $90+ = direct terms-of-trade positive
BoC Kozicki: hawkish surprise — may tighten even when economy is weak
Ivey PMI 56.6 (vs 51.1 exp) — strong domestic activity
USD/CAD fell to 1.3580 (Loonie as G10 leader)
PM Carney: cannot rule out participation in US-Israeli war with Iran
Jobs Report (FRI) is the key test for the bullish case

currencies.name: Swiss Franc
currencies.rationale: CHF retains genuine haven demand as geopolitical stress remains elevated, but the SNB has drawn a clear line in the sand at 0.9000 in EUR/CHF. The bank issued a rare explicit intervention warning on Monday (EUR/CHF touched 0.9020) and repeated it mid-week, successfully capping the Franc's appreciation and causing it to lag USD and Gold as pure havens. Swiss CPI surprised to the upside (+0.1% YoY vs -0.1% exp), moving back into positive territory and slightly complicating the SNB's deflation narrative ahead of the March 19 meeting. The weak bullish call stands while geopolitical stress persists, but gains are expected to be more constrained than the external backdrop alone would imply.
drivers: Haven demand: geopolitical stress keeps capital preservation bid alive
SNB intervention cap: explicit warning at EUR/CHF 0.9000 — limits upside
Swiss CPI +0.1% YoY (vs -0.1% exp): back in positive territory
EUR/CHF 0.9020 Monday low — rebounded to 0.9100+ on SNB jawboning
SNB March 19 meeting: CPI beat complicates dovish stance
Consumer Climate (MON) only domestic release

currencies.name: New Zealand Dollar
currencies.rationale: NZD remains the most exposed G10 currency to the current risk environment, combining a dovish RBNZ backdrop with no domestic data catalyst and full dependence on external sentiment and China-linked demand. Unlike AUD, the Kiwi had no strong local data surprise to offset the geopolitical pressure, and it largely traded as a quieter follower to AUD throughout the week. China's lowest GDP growth target since 1991 and the ongoing BHP cargo dispute weigh on the regional growth outlook that underpins NZD. With no key domestic releases this week, the currency is entirely at the mercy of global risk sentiment, conflict headlines, and China-linked commodity flows.
drivers: RBNZ dovish pivot: OCR path softer than peers — domestic yield handicap
No domestic data catalyst this week — entirely sentiment-driven
China GDP target 4.5-5%: lowest since 1991, NZD's largest trading partner
High-beta risk proxy: first cut in defensive regimes
Followed AUD lower without AUD's domestic offset (no strong GDP print)
Gold and commodity sell-off added pressure across Antipodean complex

long.rationale: Weak Bullish GBP (BoE easing priced out, gilt yields firm) vs Weak Bearish EUR (LNG shock, stagflation trap)
Weak Bullish GBP (relative rates support, BoE hawkish repricing) vs Weak Bearish JPY (energy importer, haven failure)
Weak Bullish GBP (gilt yields, BoE hawkish repricing) vs Weak Bearish NZD (RBNZ dovish, no catalyst)
Bullish CAD (petro-currency, oil windfall, hawkish BoC) vs Weak Bearish JPY (energy importer, haven failure)
Weak Bullish CHF (haven demand, SNB cap limits downside) vs Weak Bearish JPY (energy importer, haven failure)
Weak Bullish USD (haven premium, energy exporter) vs Weak Bearish JPY (haven failure, oil shock)

short.rationale: Weak Bearish EUR (LNG shock, stagflation) vs Bullish CAD (petro-currency, oil windfall)
Weak Bearish EUR (energy importer, ECB trapped) vs Weak Bullish GBP (BoE hawkish repricing)
Weak Bearish EUR (energy importer, ECB trapped) vs Weak Bullish USD (haven, energy exporter)
Weak Bearish NZD (RBNZ dovish, no catalyst) vs Bullish CAD (petro-currency, strong data)
Weak Bearish NZD (dovish RBNZ, China concerns) vs Weak Bullish USD (haven premium)
